q: For a beginner doing 100 messages/day with average 500 input + 1,000 output tokens, the realistic monthly cost of DeepSeek V4 Flash is closest to:
explain: V4 Flash at $0.10/$0.30 per 1M tokens × 100 msgs × 30 days × (500 input + 1000 output) tokens ≈ $1.20. Cheap models at beginner volume are $1–$10/month; expensive models at the same volume are $50–$250/month.

q: In the tier-routing pattern, what percentage of total calls should hit the cheap executor tier?
explain: The executor pool (V4 Flash, Kimi, M3) handles the bulk — 50 to 500 calls per task. The orchestrator (Opus) is called 1–5 times per task for planning. ~95% of call count lives in the cheap tier.

q: Which task is the WRONG fit for a cheap executor model?
choices: A 7am cron job that posts a news digest to Discord
One-shot creative writing where polish matters
explain: Creative one-shots need polish — V4 Flash tends to be verbose and miss the mark. Use Opus or Sonnet for the first draft, then route follow-ups to cheap.

q: Short answer: name the single most important defensive habit from this lesson.
explain: Set a hard monthly budget cap in your model provider's dashboard on day one. $20 for a beginner, with email alerts at 50%, 80%, 100%. Without it, a runaway session can burn $50+ in two hours.

choices: Rotate models randomly to spread the load
Use an expensive orchestrator for planning, a cheap executor pool for bulk work
Run two models in parallel on every request
explain: Tiered routing = smart/expensive for planning + hard one-shots, cheap/fast for bulk execution. It's "right tool for the right task," not "always cheap" or "always expensive."

q: True or false: output tokens cost the same as input tokens, so a model's headline input price tells you the whole story.
explain: False. Output tokens usually cost 3–5× more per token than input. A model that produces verbose output costs more than its headline input number suggests — always check the output price, not just the input.
